# Gemini Review Pass 1: Fact Checker (RAG-Grounded)

> **You are a fact-checking reviewer for Ukrainian language curriculum.**
> Your ONLY task: compare the module content against authoritative reference sources.
> You do NOT evaluate style, pedagogy, or naturalness — that is a separate reviewer's job.

**IMPORTANT: This content was generated by another AI agent. You are an independent fact-checker.
Compare claims against the provided reference sources. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge.**

---

## Module Content

```markdown
## Вступ — Introduction

Your journey began in Module 1, where you learned the full Ukrainian alphabet map — all 33 letters laid out before you. Module 2 explored the vowels and their unique sounds, and then Module 3 tackled the consonants. Now it's time for the final pieces of the puzzle.

Today you'll meet the soft sign (**Ь**) — a letter with no sound of its own that changes the consonant before it. You'll learn how the apostrophe (**'**) keeps certain sounds separate. You'll discover the affricates **Ц**, **Ч**, and **Щ**, plus the rare **Ф**. And you'll meet two digraphs — **ДЖ** and **ДЗ** — where two letters make one sound.

After this module, you can read any Ukrainian word. Every single one. Let's finish what you started!

## М'який знак — The Soft Sign

The letter **Ь** is unique in the Ukrainian alphabet — it has no sound of its own. Instead, it softens the consonant that comes before it. When you see **Ь** after a consonant, place your tongue a little closer to the roof of your mouth as you say that consonant. The result is a palatalized sound — lighter and gentler than the hard version.

### Літера Ь

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cJlal8XKBxo" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ь" />

Here are everyday words with **Ь**:

- **сіль** (salt) — the **Л** before **Ь** is soft
- **день** (day) — the **Н** before **Ь** is soft
- **Льві́в** (Lviv) — **Ь** appears before another consonant
- **мідь** (copper) — the **Д** before **Ь** is soft
- **о́сінь** (autumn) — the **Н** before **Ь** is soft
- **кінь** (horse) — the **Н** before **Ь** is soft

> [!tip] **Pattern**
> **Ь** appears after consonants at the end of a word (**сіль**, **день**) or before another consonant (**Льві́в**). It never appears at the start of a word, and never after a vowel.

Notice how **Ь** changes meaning. Compare these two words:

- **кінь** — horse
- **кін** — a stake in a game

The same letters, but **Ь** softens the final **Н**, creating an entirely different word. Your ears will learn to hear this difference with practice — don't worry if it takes time.

Here's a short exchange using your new words:

> — Добрий **день**!
> — Добрий **день**!
> — **Сіль** тут?
> — Так, **сіль** тут.

<!-- adapted from: Bolshakova, Grade 2 -->

## Апостроф — The Apostrophe

Remember from Module 2 that the iotated vowels **Я**, **Ю**, **Є**, **Ї** can represent two sounds — a **Й**-glide plus a vowel. The apostrophe (**'**) is a small but powerful mark that keeps this **Й**-sound alive.

When a consonant is followed directly by an iotated vowel, Ukrainian normally softens that consonant and absorbs the **Й**-glide. The apostrophe prevents this. It tells you: «Keep the consonant hard. Let the iotated vowel keep its full two-sound value.»

Here are key words with the apostrophe:

- **м'я́со** (meat) — hard **М** + **Й** + **А**
- **п'ять** (five) — hard **П** + **Й** + **А**
- **сім'я́** (family) — hard **М** + **Й** + **А**
- **м'яч** (ball) — hard **М** + **Й** + **А**
- **об'є́кт** (object) — hard **Б** + **Й** + **Е**
- **в'яз** (elm) — hard **В** + **Й** + **А**

> [!warning] **The Apostrophe Rule**
> The apostrophe appears after **Б**, **П**, **В**, **М**, **Ф**, **Р** before **Я**, **Ю**, **Є**, **Ї**. It is never optional — without it, the word is spelled incorrectly and sounds different.

Think of it this way: without the apostrophe, the combination **М** + **Я** would mean «soft М + А». With the apostrophe between them, it means «hard М + Й + А» instead. The apostrophe preserves the **Й**-sound you learned in Module 2. This distinction matters — leaving out the apostrophe changes both the pronunciation and the meaning of a word.

Practice reading with your new words:

> — Це **м'я́со**?
> — Так, це **м'я́со**.
> — А це **сіль**?
> — Ні, це **цу́кор**.

<!-- adapted from: Bolshakova, Grade 2 -->

## Африкати, Щ та Ф — Affricates, Щ, and Ф

Now for some sounds that might be more familiar than you expect.

### Літера Ц

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u44eCjR2Oz8" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ц" />

**Ц** is a true affricate — the sounds **Т** and **С** fused into one. You already make this sound in English: think of the «ts» at the end of «cats» or «bits». In Ukrainian, this sound can appear anywhere in a word, including the very beginning.

- **цу́кор** (sugar) — an everyday kitchen word
- **цибу́ля** (onion) — another kitchen staple

You'll also find **Ц** in common word endings, as in **хло́пець** (boy) and **вчи́телька** (teacher).

### Літера Ч

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ч" />

**Ч** is another affricate, like the «ch» in English «church». It's one of the most frequent consonants in Ukrainian.

- **час** (time, hour) — a top-100 Ukrainian word
- **черепа́ха** (turtle) — a favourite in children's stories
- **чай** (tea) — you'll hear this one every day

Try this quick exchange:

> — **Що** це?
> — Це **чай**.
> — А це?
> — Це **цу́кор**.

### Літера Щ

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QmBLieIuf6Q" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Щ" />

**Щ** looks like it should be a single sound, but it's actually a consonant cluster — two sounds, **Ш** + **Ч**, written as one letter. Say «fresh cheese» quickly — that «sh-ch» at the boundary is close to Ukrainian **Щ**.

- **що** (what) — one of the top 10 most common Ukrainian words
- **ще** (still, more)
- **ща́стя** (happiness)

> [!note] **Common Learner Mistake**
> Many learners drop the **Ч** part of **Щ** and pronounce **що** as though it were just **Ш** + **О**. Remember: **Щ** = **Ш** + **Ч**, not just **Ш**. Keep both sounds!

### Літера Ф

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=haHRsFFZRQI" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ф" />

**Ф** sounds just like English «f». It's rare in native Ukrainian words — you'll mostly find it in borrowings from other languages.

- **факт** (fact) — a familiar internationalism
- **фо́то** (photo)

**Ф** is the voiceless partner of **В**, just as **П** is the voiceless partner of **Б**.

## Диграфи ДЖ, ДЗ — Digraphs

Ukrainian has two digraphs — combinations where two letters on the page represent a single sound.

**ДЖ** sounds like the English «j» in «jungle» or «g» in «gem». It's the voiced partner of **Ч**.

- **джерело́** (spring, source) — a beautiful word for a natural spring
- **бджола́** (bee) — notice the **ДЖ** hiding inside the word

**ДЗ** has no English equivalent. It's the voiced partner of **Ц** — imagine adding voice to that «ts» sound you just learned.

- **дзвін** (bell) — Ukrainian church bells have a distinctive ring; this word is the root of **дзвіно́к** (a bell, a ring)
- **дзе́ркало** (mirror)

> [!culture] **A Uniquely Ukrainian Sound**
> The digraph **ДЗ** is a marker of authentic Ukrainian phonology. When you say **дзвін** or **дзе́ркало**, you're producing a sound that belongs to this language alone.

> [!warning] **Don't Split the Digraph**
> When you see **ДЖ** or **ДЗ**, read them as one sound. Don't read **дзвін** as «Д + З + він» — it's one smooth sound at the start.

A quick practice:

> — **Що** це?
> — Це **дзе́ркало**.
> — А це?
> — Це **м'яч**.

## Весь алфавіт! — The Full Alphabet Mastered

You did it! Here is the complete 33-letter Ukrainian alphabet:

**А Б В Г Ґ Д Е Є Ж З И І Ї Й К Л М Н О П Р С Т У Ф Х Ц Ч Ш Щ Ь Ю Я**

Plus the two digraphs **ДЖ** and **ДЗ**, and the apostrophe **'**. That's your complete toolkit for reading Ukrainian.

### Літера Ґ

<YouTube url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gNjHqjTW9WQ" title="Anna Ohoiko — Ukrainian Lessons — Ґ" />

Recall the plosive «g» sound of **Ґ** from Module 3 — as in **ґа́нок** (porch). It's the rarest letter in the alphabet, but uniquely Ukrainian.

### Reading Challenge

Try reading these sentences aloud. They use vowels, consonants, the soft sign, an apostrophe, affricates, and digraphs:

- **Моя́ сім'я́ тут.** (My family is here.)
- **Ма́ма, та́то і кіт.** (Mom, dad, and the cat.)
- **Сіль, цу́кор, чай.** (Salt, sugar, tea.)
- **Бджола́ тут!** (A bee is here!)
- **Що це? Це дзе́ркало.** (What is this? This is a mirror.)

### Survival Phrases

These phrases use your full alphabet. Read them aloud — you have all the tools you need:

- **До́брий день!** — Good day!
- **Як спра́ви?** — How are you?
- **Спаси́бі!** — Thanks!
- **Будь ла́ска!** — **Please / You're welcome!** (Note that this is a fixed phrase.)
- **До поба́чення!** — Goodbye!

You can now decode any Ukrainian word you encounter. The reading skills from Modules 1 through 4 are the foundation for everything that follows. Be proud of how far you've come!

## Підсумок — Summary

Congratulations — you've completed the alphabet! Here's what you learned:

- **Ь** (soft sign) softens the consonant before it, with no sound of its own
- The **apostrophe** preserves the **Й**-sound before iotated vowels (**Я**, **Ю**, **Є**, **Ї**)
- **Ц** and **Ч** are affricates — fused sounds
- **Щ** is a consonant cluster: **Ш** + **Ч**
- **ДЖ** and **ДЗ** are digraphs — two letters, one sound each
- **Ф** is rare in native words, common in borrowings

**Self-check — ask yourself:**

1. What does **Ь** do to the consonant before it?
2. When do you write an apostrophe?
3. What two sounds does **Щ** represent?
4. Can you read any Ukrainian word now?

If you answered yes to question 4, you're ready for the next step. Our next session, Module 5, will explore syllables and word division — how Ukrainian words break into pieces for reading and writing.
```

module: a1-004
level: A1
sequence: 4
slug: completing-the-alphabet
version: '2.0'
title: Completing the Alphabet
subtitle: "Завершуємо алфавіт — Soft Sign, Apostrophe, Affricates, and Digraphs"
focus: grammar
pedagogy: PPP
phase: A1.1 [First Contact]
word_target: 1200
objectives:
- Learner understands the soft sign (Ь) as a consonant modifier with no sound of its own
- Learner understands the apostrophe as a separator preserving the Й-sound
- "Learner can pronounce affricates Ц, Ч, Щ and digraphs ДЖ, ДЗ"
- Learner knows Ф appears mostly in borrowed words
- "Learner can read any Ukrainian word — the full 33-letter alphabet is mastered"
content_outline:
- section: Вступ — Introduction
  words: 100
  points:
  - "Review: M1 gave you the map, M2 mastered vowels, M3 mastered consonants. Today:
    the final pieces — modifiers (Ь, apostrophe), affricates (Ц Ч Щ), digraphs
    (ДЖ ДЗ), and the rare Ф. After this, you can read ANY Ukrainian word."
- section: "М'який знак — The Soft Sign"
  words: 250
  points:
  - "Ь has no sound of its own — it softens (palatalizes) the consonant before it.
    Place your tongue closer to the roof of your mouth."
  - "Words: сіль (salt), день (day), Львів (Lviv), мідь (copper), осінь (autumn)."
  - "Pattern: Ь appears after consonants at word end (сіль, день) or before another
    consonant (Львів). Never at word start, never after vowels."
  - "Minimal pair: кінь (horse) vs кін (a stake in a game) — Ь changes the preceding
    consonant's quality, creating a different word."
- section: "Апостроф — The Apostrophe"
  words: 250
  points:
  - "The apostrophe separates a consonant from a following iotated vowel (Я Ю Є Ї),
    preserving the Й-sound that would otherwise be absorbed into softening."
  - "Words: м'ясо (meat), п'ять (five), сім'я (family), м'яч (ball), об'єкт (object)."
  - "Rule: apostrophe appears after Б, П, В, М, Ф, Р before Я, Ю, Є, Ї."
  - "Compare: without apostrophe, М+Я would mean 'soft М + А'. With apostrophe,
    М'Я means 'hard М + Й + А'. The apostrophe is NOT optional."
- section: "Африкати, Щ та Ф — Affricates, Щ, and Ф"
  words: 300
  points:
  - "Ц — a true affricate: Т+С fused into one sound. Like English 'ts' in 'cats'.
    Words: цукор (sugar), цибуля (onion). Common in endings: -ець, -иця."
  - "Ч — a true affricate: like English 'ch' in 'church'. Very frequent. Words:
    час (time/hour), черепаха (turtle), чай (tea)."
  - "Щ — NOT an affricate. It represents TWO separate sounds: Ш+Ч (a consonant
    cluster written as one letter). Words: що (what), ще (still/more), щастя
    (happiness). що appears in almost every conversation."
  - "Ф — like English 'f'. Rare in native Ukrainian words — appears mostly in
    borrowings: факт (fact), фото (photo). Voiceless partner of В."
- section: "Диграфи ДЖ, ДЗ — Digraphs"
  words: 150
  points:
  - "Two letters, one sound each. These are single phonemes written with two characters."
  - "ДЖ — like English 'j' in 'jungle'. Words: джерело (spring/source),
    бджола (bee). Voiced partner of Ч."
  - "ДЗ — no English equivalent. Voiced partner of Ц. Words: дзвін (bell),
    дзеркало (mirror). Uniquely Ukrainian — absent from Russian."
- section: "Весь алфавіт! — The Full Alphabet Mastered"
  words: 150
  points:
  - "The complete 33-letter Ukrainian alphabet: А Б В Г Ґ Д Е Є Ж З И І Ї Й К Л М
    Н О П Р С Т У Ф Х Ц Ч Ш Щ Ь Ю Я. Plus digraphs ДЖ, ДЗ and the apostrophe."
  - "Full-alphabet reading challenge: a short paragraph using all letter types —
    vowels, consonants, soft sign, apostrophe, affricates, digraphs."
  - "Survival phrases using the full alphabet: Добрий день! (Good day!) Як справи?
    (How are you?) Дякую! (Thank you!) Будь ласка! (Please!) До побачення! (Goodbye!)"
  - "Celebration: you can now decode any Ukrainian word. The reading skills from
    M1-M4 are the foundation for everything that follows."
- section: "Підсумок — Summary"
  words: 100
  points:
  - "Recap: Ь softens consonants, apostrophe preserves Й-sound, Ц and Ч are
    affricates, Щ is a Ш+Ч cluster, ДЖ and ДЗ are digraphs, Ф is rare."
  - "Self-check: What does Ь do? When do you use an apostrophe? What two sounds
    does Щ represent? Can you read any Ukrainian word now?"
  - "Next: M5 — syllables and word division."

  - "день (day) — demonstrates Ь; top 50 word; collocation: добрий день"

## Activities YAML

```yaml
- type: watch-and-repeat
  title: 'Watch and Repeat: New Letters and Sounds'
  instruction: Watch Anna Ohoiko demonstrate each letter. Pause the video and repeat
    the sound aloud.
  items:
  - letter: Ь
    word: сіль
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cJlal8XKBxo
    note: The soft sign has no sound of its own — it softens the consonant before
      it
  - letter: Ц
    word: цукор
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=u44eCjR2Oz8
    note: Like the ts in cats — but can appear at the start of a word
  - letter: Ч
    word: час
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UsJkbdsY2RA
    note: Like English ch in church
  - letter: Щ
    word: що
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QmBLieIuf6Q
    note: 'Two sounds fused together: Ш + Ч — keep both sounds!'
  - letter: Ф
    word: факт
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=haHRsFFZRQI
    note: Like English f — rare in native Ukrainian words
  - letter: Ґ
    word: ґанок
    video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gNjHqjTW9WQ
    note: The rarest letter in the alphabet — a hard g sound, uniquely Ukrainian
- type: classify
  title: Spot the Soft Sign
  instruction: Sort these words by which consonant the soft sign softens.
  categories:
  - label: Softened Н
    items:
    - день
    - осінь
    - кінь
  - label: Softened Л
    items:
    - сіль
    - Львів
    - вчителька
  - label: Softened Д
    items:
    - мідь
  - label: Softened Ц
    items:
    - хлопець
- type: image-to-letter
  title: Picture to Letter
  instruction: Look at the picture. Which special letter or digraph does the Ukrainian
    word contain?
  items:
  - emoji: 🍬
    answer: Ц
    distractors:
    - Ч
    - Щ
    note: цукор (sugar)
  - emoji: 🧅
    answer: Ц
    distractors:
    - Ч
    - Ф
    note: цибуля (onion)
  - emoji: ⏰
    answer: Ч
    distractors:
    - Ц
    - Щ
    note: час (time)
  - emoji: 🐢
    answer: Ч
    distractors:
    - Ц
    - Щ
    note: черепаха (turtle)
  - emoji: 🫖
    answer: Ч
    distractors:
    - Ц
    - Ф
    note: чай (tea)
  - emoji: 💧
    answer: ДЖ
    distractors:
    - ДЗ
    - Ч
    note: джерело (spring, source)
  - emoji: 🐝
    answer: ДЖ
    distractors:
    - ДЗ
    - Щ
    note: бджола (bee)
  - emoji: 🔔
    answer: ДЗ
    distractors:
    - ДЖ
    - Ц
    note: дзвін (bell)
- type: quiz
  title: Apostrophe Rules
  instruction: Test your knowledge of the Ukrainian apostrophe.
  items:
  - question: Which spelling of the word for 'meat' is correct?
    options:
    - text: м'ясо
      correct: true
    - text: мясо
      correct: false
    - text: мьясо
      correct: false
    - text: м'асо
      correct: false
    explanation: 'The apostrophe after М preserves the Й-sound: hard М + Й + А.'
  - question: Which spelling of the word for 'five' is correct?
    options:
    - text: п'ять
      correct: true
    - text: пять
      correct: false
    - text: пьять
      correct: false
    - text: піять
      correct: false
    explanation: After П, the apostrophe preserves the Й-glide before Я.
  - question: Which spelling of the word for 'family' is correct?
    options:
    - text: сім'я
      correct: true
    - text: сімья
      correct: false
    - text: сімя
      correct: false
    - text: сім'а
      correct: false
    explanation: The apostrophe separates М from Я, keeping the Й-sound.
  - question: Which spelling of the word for 'ball' is correct?
    options:
    - text: м'яч
      correct: true
    - text: мяч
      correct: false
    - text: мьяч
      correct: false
    - text: міяч
      correct: false
    explanation: Same pattern as м'ясо — apostrophe after М before Я.
  - question: The apostrophe appears after which consonants in Ukrainian?
    options:
    - text: Б, П, В, М, Ф, Р
      correct: true
    - text: Б, К, Г, Д
      correct: false
    - text: All consonants
      correct: false
    - text: Only М and П
      correct: false
    explanation: 'Remember the six consonants: Б, П, В, М, Ф, Р — before iotated vowels.'
  - question: Before which vowels does the apostrophe appear?
    options:
    - text: Я, Ю, Є, Ї
      correct: true
    - text: А, О, У, И
      correct: false
    - text: All vowels
      correct: false
    - text: Only Я and Ю
      correct: false
    explanation: 'The apostrophe appears before iotated vowels: Я, Ю, Є, Ї.'
  - question: What does the apostrophe do in Ukrainian?
    options:
    - text: Preserves the Й-sound before iotated vowels
      correct: true
    - text: Softens the consonant before it
      correct: false
    - text: Adds a pause between syllables
      correct: false
    - text: Marks the stressed syllable
      correct: false
    explanation: Without the apostrophe, the consonant would soften and absorb the
      Й-glide.
  - question: Which of these words needs an apostrophe?
    options:
    - text: об'єкт
      correct: true
    - text: цукор
      correct: false
    - text: сіль
      correct: false
    - text: чай
      correct: false
    explanation: Б is one of the six consonants that take an apostrophe before Є.
  - question: Which word does NOT need an apostrophe?
    options:
    - text: день
      correct: true
    - text: м'ясо
      correct: false
    - text: п'ять
      correct: false
    - text: сім'я
      correct: false
    explanation: День has a soft sign, not an apostrophe. The apostrophe only appears
      before iotated vowels.
  - question: In the word м'ясо, the apostrophe tells you that...
    options:
    - text: М stays hard, followed by Й+А sounds
      correct: true
    - text: М becomes soft, followed by А
      correct: false
    - text: М is silent
      correct: false
    - text: The word has two syllables
      correct: false
    explanation: The apostrophe keeps М hard and preserves the full Й+А value of Я.
- type: classify
  title: Which Affricate?
  instruction: Sort these words by which affricate or consonant cluster they contain.
  categories:
  - label: Contains Ц
    items:
    - цукор
    - цибуля
    - хлопець
  - label: Contains Ч
    items:
    - час
    - черепаха
    - чай
  - label: Contains Щ
    items:
    - що
    - ще
    - щастя
- type: fill-in
  title: Complete the Survival Phrase
  instruction: Fill in the missing word to complete each survival phrase.
  items:
  - sentence: Добрий ___!
    answer: день
    options:
    - день
    - ніч
    - ранок
    - час
    explanation: Добрий день means Good day — the most common Ukrainian greeting.
  - sentence: Як ___?
    answer: справи
    options:
    - справи
    - день
    - тут
    - добре
    explanation: Як справи means How are you — a standard follow-up to a greeting.
  - sentence: ___!
    answer: Спасибі
    options:
    - Спасибі
    - Будь ласка
    - Добрий день
    - Так
    explanation: Спасибі means Thanks — one of the first words every learner needs.
  - sentence: Будь ___!
    answer: ласка
    options:
    - ласка
    - день
    - добре
    - так
    explanation: Будь ласка means Please or You're welcome — a fixed phrase.
  - sentence: До ___!
    answer: побачення
    options:
    - побачення
    - дня
    - ранку
    - вечора
    explanation: До побачення means Goodbye — literally until seeing (each other again).
  - sentence: ___ це?
    answer: Що
    options:
    - Що
    - Як
    - Де
    - Хто
    explanation: Що це means What is this — uses the letter Щ you just learned.
- type: match-up
  title: Match the Survival Phrase
  instruction: Match each Ukrainian phrase with its English meaning.
  pairs:
  - left: Добрий день!
    right: Good day!
  - left: Як справи?
    right: How are you?
  - left: Спасибі!
    right: Thanks!
  - left: Будь ласка!
    right: Please / You're welcome!
  - left: До побачення!
    right: Goodbye!
  - left: Що це?
    right: What is this?
- type: anagram
  title: Unscramble the Word
  instruction: Rearrange the letters to form a Ukrainian word from this lesson.
  items:
  - scrambled: л ь с і
    answer: сіль
  - scrambled: н ь е д
    answer: день
  - scrambled: с а ч
    answer: час
  - scrambled: й а ч
    answer: чай
  - scrambled: р о к у ц
    answer: цукор
  - scrambled: н і в з д
    answer: дзвін
  - scrambled: т к а ф
    answer: факт
  - scrambled: ь н і с о
    answer: осінь

```

---

## Research Notes

# Дослідження: Completing the Alphabet

## State Standard Reference
§4.1.1: "Ukrainian alphabet" — full alphabet recognition and ordering
§4.1.2: "Apostrophe usage" — apostrophe as separator before iotated vowels
§4.1.3: "Soft sign (м'який знак)" — palatalization modifier with no independent sound
Alignment: This module directly addresses three State Standard 2024 literacy foundations (§4.1.1–4.1.3) in a single focused lesson, completing the alphabet mastery arc begun in M1–M3.

---

## Vocabulary Frequency

All 19 items VESUM-verified ✅

| Word | VESUM POS | Notes | Key collocations |
|------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| сіль | noun | Everyday kitchen word; demonstrates final Ь | дрібка солі, морська сіль |
| день | noun | Top 50 frequency; demonstrates final Ь | добрий день, цілий день |
| м'ясо | noun | Everyday food; apostrophe demo word | свіже м'ясо, м'ясо і риба |
| п'ять | numeral | Demonstrates apostrophe; number 5 | п'ять хвилин |
| сім'я | noun | High frequency; apostrophe mid-word | наша сім'я, велика сім'я |
| цукор | noun | Kitchen word; demonstrates Ц | ложка цукру |
| час | noun | Top 100; demonstrates Ч | скільки часу?, вільний час |
| що | conj/noun | Top 10 word; demonstrates Щ | що це?, що таке? |
| джерело | noun | Demonstrates ДЖ digraph; also figurative "source" | чисте джерело |
| дзвін | noun | Demonstrates ДЗ; church bells = cultural anchor | церковний дзвін |
| осінь | noun | Seasonal; demonstrates Ь | рання осінь |
| м'яч | noun | Children's vocabulary; apostrophe | футбольний м'яч |
| щастя | noun | High frequency; demonstrates Щ | велике щастя |
| факт | noun | Internationalism; demonstrates Ф | цікавий факт |
| бджола | noun | Nature; demonstrates ДЖ | робоча бджола |
| дзеркало | noun | Everyday object; demonstrates ДЗ | велике дзеркало |
| кінь | noun | Ь minimal pair with кін; folk culture | білий кінь |
| цибуля | noun | Kitchen word; demonstrates Ц | смажена цибуля |
| чай | noun | High frequency drink; demonstrates Ч | чашка чаю, зелений чай |

---

## Cultural Hooks

1. **Church bells (дзвін)** — Ukrainian church bells have a distinctive cultural role; the word дзвін also gives the verb дзвонити (to ring/call). The ДЗ digraph is uniquely Ukrainian — absent from Russian — making it a marker of authentic Ukrainian phonology. Textbooks use this word when introducing ДЗ.

2. **Скоромовка for Щ** — «Щука в ставку, штука на плоту» (A pike in the pond, a trick on the raft). This classic tongue-twister targets Щ, ШТ, and ст clusters. Kravtsova-style textbooks use скоромовки systematically for affricate drilling.

3. **Загадка for Ц** — «Не годинник, а цокає» (Not a clock, but it ticks). Answer: цвіркун (cricket). Загадки with Ц-initial words appear in grade 3–4 textbooks (Большакова, Vashulenko) as phoneme recognition warm-ups.

---

## Common Learner Errors

1. **Soft sign omitted or misread** → «день» read as «ден» — English has no palatalization; learners treat Ь as silent but don't modify the preceding consonant. Kravtsova Grade 3 exercises contrast Ь-final words with hard-final equivalents (кінь vs кін).

2. **Apostrophe treated as decorative punctuation** → «мясо» written/read as «мясо» without separator effect — learners skip the Й-insertion. The plan's quiz activity (does this word need an apostrophe?) directly targets this. Vashulenko Grade 3 §5 exercises on апостроф use fill-in-the-blank exactly as the plan specifies.

3. **Щ pronounced as Ш** → «що» → «шо» — Ukrainian Щ = Ш+Ч, not single Ш. English "sh" is the closest English sound, so learners collapse the distinction. Kravtsova Grade 3 p.142 explicitly lists «Правильна вимова слів зі звуком...» as a separate section — this is a known high-error item.

4. **ДЗ digraph split** → «дзвін» read as Д+З separately → «дзвін» sounds wrong — learners see two letters and apply two sounds. Must be taught as one phoneme (the voiced partner of Ц), analogous to how ДЖ is the voiced partner of Ч.

---

## Cross-References

- **Builds on**: a1-001 (The Cyrillic Code I — alphabet map), a1-002 (Vowel sounds), a1-003 (Consonant sounds)
- **Prepares for**: a1-005 (Syllables and Word Division — requires knowing which consonants soften), a1-006+ (all reading-dependent modules rely on full alphabet mastery)

---

## Teaching Strategy

- **Discovery exercise**: Show the learner three word pairs on a card:
  - «сол» / «сіль» — salt (wrong form) vs salt (correct)
  - «мясо» / «м'ясо» — meat (wrong) vs meat (correct)
  - «цо» / «що» — (nonsense) vs what
  Ask: "What's different between column 1 and column 2?" Learners notice Ь, apostrophe, and Щ before the rule is stated. This mirrors Kravtsova Grade 4 p.9 "Розташування слів за алфавітом" inductive approach.

- **Best context**: Kitchen/home vocabulary is the natural anchor — сіль, цукор, м'ясо, цибуля, чай all appear in the same real-life context (shopping, cooking). Group Ь and apostrophe examples around food words.

- **Contrastive pairs**:
  - Ь: кінь (horse) vs кін (a game stake) — same letters, different meaning
  - Apostrophe: м'яч (ball, hard М + ЙА) vs м'який (soft, different word base)
  - Щ vs Ш: що (what) vs шо (dialectal/colloquial, never written in standard Ukrainian)
  - ДЗ vs Ц: дзвін (bell, voiced) vs цвіт (blossom, voiceless) — voiced/voiceless partner pattern

## Notes for Content Writing

- This is M4 — still pre-verbal phase. All classroom instructions in English. No imperative forms anywhere.
- The plan's survival phrases (Добрий день!, Дякую!, Будь ласка!) appear in the closing section — present them as **read-aloud labels**, not as communicative structures learners must produce.
- Ф section should be brief (it's rare) — the plan allocates it as sub-item of the affricates section, not a standalone section. Don't over-expand it.
- Bilingual headers required (M1–14 rule from A1 quick-ref): e.g., `## М'який знак — The Soft Sign`
- The plan's word budget is tight per section (250 for Ь, 250 for apostrophe) — don't exceed by front-loading examples; spread vocabulary across the reading flow.
- дзвін is a strong cultural anchor for ДЗ: it's uniquely Ukrainian (absent from Russian) — a one-sentence cultural note here adds decolonization value without expanding into a full framing.

## Your Task: Plan Adherence + Factual Verification

### PART A: Plan Adherence Check (MANDATORY)

The plan YAML above is the source of truth for what the module MUST contain. Check EVERY point systematically:

#### A1. Content Outline Points

For EACH section in `content_outline`, and for EACH `points` entry within that section:
1. Search the module content for evidence that this point is addressed
2. Classify as:
   - **COVERED** — the point is clearly addressed in the content. Quote the relevant sentence(s).
   - **MISSING** — the point does not appear in the content. This is a **MEDIUM** severity issue.
   - **PARTIAL** — some aspect is covered but key details are missing. Note what's missing.

**Output a checklist:**
```
### Plan Point Coverage
- Section "Вступ — Introduction":
  - [x] Point 1: "A1 immersion 10-50%..." — COVERED (line ~5: "...")
  - [ ] Point 2: "Cultural hook: Cyrillic script..." — MISSING
  ...
```

#### A2. Required Vocabulary

For EACH word in `vocabulary_hints.required`:
1. Search the module content (not just the word list section — the entire prose)
2. Check if it appears in the activities YAML (if provided in the content above)
3. Classify as:
   - **PRESENT** — found in prose AND activities
   - **PROSE ONLY** — in prose but not in any activity (LOW)
   - **MISSING** — not in prose at all (**HIGH** severity)

#### A3. Activity Hints

For EACH entry in `activity_hints`:
1. Check if an activity of that `type` exists
2. Check if the `focus` matches
3. Check if the `items` count is met
4. Flag missing activity types as **MEDIUM**

#### A4. Section Word Budgets

For EACH section in `content_outline` with a `words` target:
1. Estimate the word count of the corresponding content section
2. Flag sections >10% under their target as **MEDIUM**

#### A5. Pronunciation Videos (if plan has `pronunciation_videos`)

Check that video links from the plan are embedded in the content. Flag missing embeds as **MEDIUM**.

---

### PART B: Factual Verification

For EVERY grammar explanation, factual claim, cultural assertion, and historical reference in the module content:

1. **Search the reference sources above** for confirming or contradicting evidence
2. **Classify each claim** as one of:
   - **CONFIRMED [Tier 1]** — matches a reference source. Cite the source (chunk ID or source identifier) and quote both the module claim and the reference.
   - **DISCREPANCY [Tier 1]** — contradicts a reference source. HIGH severity. Cite both the module claim and the contradicting reference with chunk ID. Suggest the correct version.
   - **UNVERIFIED** — no reference coverage. Flag explicitly. Do NOT evaluate from your own knowledge. Do NOT mark as correct or incorrect — just "UNVERIFIED".

### Citation Enforcement

Every DISCREPANCY entry MUST include:
- Exact quote from the module content
- Exact quote from the reference source
- **Chunk ID or source identifier** (e.g., "Textbook, Grade 3, chunk_abc123" or "VESUM: lemma X")

Free-text citations with no chunk ID or source identifier carry ZERO weight and will be programmatically discarded.

### Special Checks

1. **Callout boxes** (`[!did-you-know]`, `[!myth-buster]`, `[!culture-note]`, `[!fun-fact]`): These are the #1 source of fabrication. Scrutinize every claim.
2. **Grammar rules**: Verify rule statements against textbook references. Flag overgeneralizations.
3. **Etymologies**: Verify word origins against references. Flag folk etymologies.
4. **Statistics and dates**: Flag unsourced numbers.
5. **VESUM failures**: Words not found in VESUM may be misspellings or Russianisms.

---

## Output Format

You MUST use these exact delimiters:

```
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_START===
## Plan Adherence Summary

**Content outline points:** N total, N covered, N missing, N partial
**Required vocabulary:** N total, N present, N missing
**Activity hints:** N total, N matched, N missing
**Plan Adherence Score:** X/10
(10 = all points covered; deduct 1 per MISSING content point, 2 per MISSING required word)

### Plan Point Coverage

[Checklist from Part A1 — every point with [x] or [ ] and evidence]

### Required Vocabulary Coverage

| Word | In Prose? | In Activities? | Status |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| ... | YES/NO | YES/NO | PRESENT / PROSE ONLY / MISSING |

### Activity Hints Coverage

| Hint Type | Focus | Required Items | Found? | Actual Items |
|-----------|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|
| ... | ... | N | YES/NO | N |

### Missing Plan Points (Fix Targets)

[List each MISSING or PARTIAL point with the exact plan text and which section it belongs to.
These become fix targets in the SECTION_FIX block below.]

---

## Factual Verification Summary

**Total claims checked:** N
**Confirmed [Tier 1]:** N
**Discrepancies [Tier 1]:** N
**Unverified:** N
**Factual Alignment Score:** X/10

## Confirmed Claims

[List confirmed claims with source citations]

## Discrepancies

### Discrepancy 1: [brief title]
- **Module says:** "[exact quote from module]"
- **Reference says:** "[exact quote from reference source]"
- **Source:** [chunk ID / source identifier]
- **Severity:** HIGH
- **Suggested fix:** [what to change]

[... more discrepancies ...]

## Unverified Claims

[List claims with no reference coverage]

## Verdict

**Status:** PASS / FAIL
(FAIL if any HIGH severity discrepancy OR 3+ MISSING plan points found)
===FACTUAL_REVIEW_END===
```

If you find fixable discrepancies, also output FIND/REPLACE pairs:

```
===SECTION_FIX_START===
FILE: /Users/krisztiankoos/projects/learn-ukrainian/curriculum/l2-uk-en/a1/completing-the-alphabet.md
FIND:
[exact text from module with the error]
REPLACE:
[corrected text based on reference source]
---
[... more pairs, max 15 ...]
===SECTION_FIX_END===
```

---

## Boundaries

- Do NOT evaluate style, naturalness, or pedagogy — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT score dimensions (except Plan Adherence and Factual Alignment) — that is Pass 2's job
- Do NOT evaluate factual claims from your own knowledge — ONLY compare against provided references
- Plan adherence checking uses the plan YAML as ground truth — this is objective, not subjective
- Do NOT fabricate references — if no reference covers a claim, mark it UNVERIFIED
- Maximum 15 FIND/REPLACE pairs (prioritize MISSING plan points over minor factual fixes)
